chunk_summary: This section discusses formula selection and step-up escalation strategies for non-breastfed infants with suspected cow's milk protein allergy, focusing on diagnostic elimination and de-labeling approaches.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic elimination process for suspected cow's milk protein allergy in non-breastfed infants.
Identify appropriate formula options for infants with cow's milk protein allergy.
Learn step-up escalation strategies when initial formula interventions fail.
Review guidelines for de-labeling cow's milk protein allergy in infants.
key_facts: Extensively hydrolyzed formulas (eHF) are the first-line choice for non-breastfed infants with suspected cow's milk protein allergy.
Amino acid-based formulas (AAF) are recommended for severe cases or when eHF fails.
Diagnostic elimination diets should be trialed for 2-4 weeks to assess symptom resolution.
Reintroduction of cow's milk protein under medical supervision is essential for de-labeling.
The prevalence of cow's milk protein allergy in infants is estimated to be 2-3% globally.
Guidelines emphasize the importance of avoiding unnecessary dietary restrictions to prevent nutritional deficiencies.
reference_quote: Extensively hydrolyzed formulas (eHF) are recommended as the first-line treatment for non-breastfed infants with suspected cow's milk protein allergy, with amino acid-based formulas (AAF) reserved for severe or refractory cases. (Source: BSACI guideline for diagnosis and management of cow’s milk allergy)
document_key_topics: Formula selection for cow's milk protein allergy
Step-up escalation strategies in non-breastfed infants
Guidelines for managing cow's milk protein allergy
